title: if / elif / else
summary: Learn how to make your Python programs make decisions using if, elif, and else — the building blocks of all program logic.

blocks.md:
Every useful program needs to make decisions. Should we show an error message or a success message? Is the user old enough? Did they guess the right number?

In Python, we use **if statements** to ask a yes-or-no question and run different code depending on the answer. This is one of the most important ideas in all of programming.

An `if` statement checks whether a condition is **True**. If it is, Python runs the indented block of code beneath it. If not, Python skips it entirely.

The structure always looks like this:

```
if condition:
    do something
```

Notice the **colon** at the end of the `if` line, and the **4-space indent** on the next line. Both are required.

blocks.code:
temperature = 35

if temperature > 30:
    print("It's hot outside!")

print("Done checking the weather.")
blocks.output:
It's hot outside!
Done checking the weather.
blocks.caption: The message prints because 35 > 30 is True.

blocks.title: Think of it like a gate
blocks.md: Imagine a bouncer at a club. They check your ID. **If** you're old enough, you get in. The `if` statement is that gate — only the code inside the indented block runs when the condition passes.

Python uses **indentation** (spaces at the start of a line) to know which lines belong inside an `if` block. Most Python code uses **4 spaces** per level. This is not just style — it changes how your program behaves.

blocks.title: Indentation errors will crash your program
blocks.md: Forgetting to indent, or mixing tabs and spaces, causes an `IndentationError`. Always use **4 spaces** (not a tab character) for each level. Most editors handle this for you automatically.

blocks.code:
score = 45

if score >= 50:
    print("You passed!")   # indented — inside the if
    print("Great work.")   # also inside the if

print("Thanks for playing.")  # NOT indented — always runs
blocks.caption: score is 45, so the indented lines are skipped. The last line always runs.

blocks.md: Sometimes you want to do one thing if a condition is true, and a *different* thing if it is false. That is exactly what `else` is for. It runs when the `if` condition is **False**.

blocks.caption: 16 >= 18 is False, so the else block runs.

blocks.md: What if you have more than two possibilities? Use **`elif`** (short for "else if") to check additional conditions, one after another. Python stops at the first one that is True.

blocks.caption: Python checks each condition top to bottom and stops at the first True one.

blocks.title: Order matters with elif
blocks.md: Python checks conditions **in order** and stops as soon as one matches. Put your most specific conditions first. If you put `grade >= 70` before `grade >= 90`, a score of 95 would print `"C"` — not what you want!

Python does not only accept `True` or `False` in an `if` condition. Many values are treated as **truthy** (count as True) or **falsy** (count as False).

- **Falsy**: `0`, `""` (empty string), `[]` (empty list), `None`
- **Truthy**: any non-zero number, any non-empty string or list

This lets you write very clean, readable checks.

blocks.caption: An empty string is falsy, so the else branch runs.

blocks.md: You can put an `if` statement *inside* another `if` block. This is called **nesting**. It is useful when you need to check one thing only after a first condition passes.

blocks.output:
Welcome!
Head to the general area.
blocks.caption: The inner if only runs because has_ticket is True.

blocks.title: Don't nest too deeply
blocks.md: More than 2-3 levels of nesting makes code hard to read. If you find yourself indenting 5 times, look for a way to simplify using `elif` or by breaking logic into separate functions.

Python has a compact way to write a simple if/else in a single line, called a **conditional expression** (sometimes called a ternary expression). It follows this pattern:

```
value = thing_if_true if condition else thing_if_false
```

Use this only for simple choices — it can hurt readability if overused.

blocks.code:
points = 120
label = "Winner" if points >= 100 else "Keep trying"
print(label)
blocks.caption: A concise way to assign a value based on a condition.

blocks.explanation: x is 10. The first condition (10 > 20) is False, so Python moves to elif. The second condition (10 > 5) is True, so it prints "medium" and stops — it never reaches the else.

keyTakeaways: Use `if condition:` (with a colon) to run code only when a condition is True.
Always indent the code block inside an `if`, `elif`, or `else` by 4 spaces — indentation is how Python knows what belongs inside.
`elif` lets you check multiple conditions in order; Python stops at the first True one.
`else` is the fallback that runs when no previous condition matched.
For a simple two-way choice in one line, use the conditional expression: `a if condition else b`.

exercise.prompt: Write a program that asks for a number (you can just assign it directly, no need for input) and prints: "Positive" if it is greater than 0, "Negative" if it is less than 0, or "Zero" if it equals 0. Then, on the same run, also print "Even" or "Odd" for non-zero numbers (you can skip this check for zero).
exercise.starterCode:
number = 7  # try changing this value

# Your if / elif / else logic here
exercise.hint: Use the modulo operator `%` to check if a number is even: `number % 2 == 0` is True when it divides evenly by 2. You can nest a second if/else inside the first branch.
